title: The Raincoats (1)

jokes.text: Don't you hate these people that talk into your mouth...like you're a clown at a drive-through?
jokes.setup: Jerry discussing conversational distance problems
jokes.punchline: Comparing close talkers to drive-through clown interaction
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor with vivid, absurd imagery. The drive-through clown comparison is unexpected and visual.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like ordering from a drive-through clown. You know, those clown's mouth where you order the food? I'm at the clown's mouth, the guy's inside the clown, he's talking to me, I can feel the spray coming out of his mouth on my face.

jokes.text: Sometimes they give you...the three-quarter handshake, just the fingers.
jokes.setup: Handshake guidelines discussion
jokes.punchline: Specific description of awkward partial handshake
jokes.explanation: Good observational detail that many can relate to, though not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A handshake is a very specific thing. You can't just do whatever you want. There are rules and regulations.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You go in, firm grip, two pumps, out. That's it. You don't improvise.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Two pumps?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Two pumps. In, out. You can't be doing three pumps, you can't be doing a half pump. You certainly can't be going to the elbow.

jokes.text: I have actually said to people, 'Hey, the handshake is over.'
jokes.setup: People disputing handshake releases
jokes.punchline: Jerry's direct confrontation about handshake completion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry character moment - overthinking social situations to the point of awkward directness. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what bothers me? When you're shaking hands with someone and they won't let go. How do you end a handshake? There's no rules! It's like a kiss - at some point one person has to pull away first. And if they don't, it becomes uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I was shaking hands with a guy the other day, and I'm thinking, 'Okay, this is long enough. Time to disengage.' But he's still pumping! He's not done yet. So I have to keep going, like some kind of human hydraulic pump.

jokes.text: That's three strikes. You're out.
jokes.setup: Listing multiple handshake violations
jokes.punchline: Baseball metaphor for social interaction failure
jokes.explanation: Nice callback structure and economy, though the baseball metaphor is somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, a handshake is like a baseball game. You got your grip, that's your stance. You got your eye contact, that's your pitch. And if you mess up any of those things, you're out. You're just out of the game. No second chances. The handshake doesn't lie.

jokes.text: I'm getting a little backed up.
jokes.setup: Jerry hasn't been with Rachel for three weeks
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic way of describing sexual frustration
jokes.explanation: Mild sexual humor delivered in Jerry's understated style, though not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I haven't been with Rachel for three weeks. Three weeks! You know what that means? I'm starting to believe in the supernatural.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I saw a ghost yesterday. Just floating by my apartment. And I thought, 'That's nice. At least someone's visiting me.'

jokes.text: Maybe this will become, like, a cool thing, living with your parents. Then maybe baldness will catch on.
jokes.setup: George trying to rationalize living with parents
jokes.punchline: Connecting it to his baldness also becoming trendy
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - delusional optimism about his obvious flaws. The baldness connection is unexpected and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My parents want me to move out. But I'm thinking, why should I? The rent is free, my mother does my laundry, I get three meals a day. It's a win-win situation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Besides, look at me. I'm bald. A few years ago, this was a death sentence. Now it's trendy. Guys are shaving their heads on purpose. So if baldness can make a comeback, why can't living with your parents?

jokes.text: When the aliens come, who do you think they're gonna relate to? Who do you think's gonna be getting tour of the ship? The baldies.
jokes.setup: George claiming baldness will catch on
jokes.punchline: Elaborate alien theory favoring bald people
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George escalation - taking his delusion to cosmic proportions. The specificity of 'tour of the ship' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, it's going to happen. Mark my words - baldness is going to be the biggest trend of the '90s. Everyone's going to want to be bald.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, no one wants to be bald.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, you think so? Well, what if I told you that extraterrestrials have already begun colonizing Earth, and they specifically prefer bald people because it makes us aerodynamic for intergalactic travel? They're going to come down, see all these bald guys, and think we're one of them. By the time anyone figures it out, it'll be too late.

jokes.text: Did you see the mug on that kid?
jokes.setup: George agreeing to be Joey's Big Brother
jokes.punchline: Immediately insulting the child's appearance
jokes.explanation: Perfect George cruelty - agreeing to help then immediately being awful. Character-driven humor.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what? I'll be your Big Brother.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Really? That's great!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah. So, uh, what's the deal with that shirt? That's a terrible color on you. And those pants? Forget about it. You got a real uphill battle here, kid.

jokes.text: Wouldn't you like to pass the ketchup to someone like me? Please?
jokes.setup: Mocking Joey's manipulative plea technique
jokes.punchline: Applying it to mundane ketchup request
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that deconstructs Joey's manipulation by applying it to something trivial.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So you just look at them with these big eyes and say, 'Please, I really need this'?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Can you pass the ketchup?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Please... I really need this ketchup. My life depends on it.

jokes.text: Have you noticed they moved...where they do the interview on Jeopardy? Yeah. It was in the middle of Single Jeopardy. Now it's after Single Jeopardy.
jokes.setup: Casual conversation starter
jokes.punchline: Obsessive attention to trivial TV format change
jokes.explanation: Good 'show about nothing' moment - finding deep meaning in TV trivia. Very Seinfeld-esque.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Did you notice they changed the opening credits?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean changed? They're the same.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, the font is different. It's slightly more... slanted.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's the same font Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're not looking close enough. This has been bothering me all week.

jokes.text: Then all I have to do is buy postcards...and have your parents mail them from Paris.
jokes.setup: George wants to fake being in Paris
jokes.punchline: Elaborate deception involving Jerry's parents as unwitting accomplices
jokes.explanation: Classic George scheme - elaborate, selfish, and using others. Shows his devious creativity.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of telling everyone I went to Paris.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You didn't go to Paris.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but I could say I did. I could get some postcards, leave them around.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, hear me out. Your parents could back me up. They could say they ran into me at the airport.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why would my parents do that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because they don't know what you're talking about. We don't even have to tell them. They'll just naturally say something that helps my story.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: This is the worst idea you've ever had.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's foolproof.

jokes.text: He's probably better off.
jokes.setup: George abandoning his Big Brother commitment
jokes.punchline: Dry agreement that the child benefits from George's neglect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry deadpan - acknowledging George's toxicity with understated agreement.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're not going to see your Little Brother anymore?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I don't think so.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I think that's good. I think the kid's better off.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah. I mean, what are you gonna do? Take him to a movie? Buy him a soda? He's better off without it.

jokes.text: Because George...is a deeply disturbed individual.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking why George is faking Paris trip
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact psychological diagnosis
jokes.explanation: Jerry's clinical assessment of George delivered with perfect deadpan timing. Quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why are you pretending to go to Paris?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's a compulsive liar. It's a sickness.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not a compulsive liar!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You just lied about Paris.

jokes.text: Yeah, or if ever on Fifth Avenue here in New York City.
jokes.setup: Helen mentioning Saks Fifth Avenue in Miami
jokes.punchline: Morty's obvious correction about the original location
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Morty's pedantic nature - correcting obvious geographic facts.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: We went to Saks Fifth Avenue in Miami.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Saks Fifth Avenue is in New York!

jokes.text: See, when people like you die...the widows, they bring in their wardrobes.
jokes.setup: Explaining used clothing store business
jokes.punchline: Casually matter-of-fact about profiting from death
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny business explanation that treats death as a supply chain issue. Good character-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well I'm starting a new business. I'm gonna buy clothing off the dead.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Off the dead?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, when somebody dies, they don't need their clothes anymore. So I go to the morgue, I get the clothes, I clean 'em up, and I sell 'em.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's a great idea, Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. I'm gonna make a killing.

jokes.text: What happens if the husband dies after the wife? Who brings the clothing in then? I suppose the children do. Yes, I suppose they do.
jokes.setup: Helen's logical follow-up about death scenarios
jokes.punchline: Uncomfortable realization about family death logistics
jokes.explanation: Awkward moment where death logistics become too real. The repetition shows the discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: So what happens if we both die at the same time?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'what happens'? You're dead, I'm dead, we're both dead.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: But who calls the police?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? If we're both dead, nobody calls anybody!

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Well somebody has to find us eventually.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Oh sure, they'll find us. Weeks later. Decomposed. The smell will bring the neighbors.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Frank, that's horrible!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What do you want me to say? We'll leave a note?

jokes.text: What plans? We have plans. Where did you get plans? We have plans.
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents declining dinner invitation
jokes.punchline: Increasingly vague and defensive about mysterious plans
jokes.explanation: Good escalating repetition showing the parents' obvious discomfort with lying.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: We can't make it tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: We have something we have to do.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: It's very important.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: We've had it planned for weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You wouldn't understand.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: It's not something we can talk about.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Let's just say we'll be... occupied.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: With things. Important things.

jokes.text: Jerry, we don't care much for the Costanzas. We can't stand them.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking why they won't have dinner
jokes.punchline: Blunt admission of hatred for the Costanzas
jokes.explanation: Helen's uncharacteristic directness is shocking and funny. The escalation from 'don't care much for' to 'can't stand' works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why won't you have dinner with the Costanzas?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Because we hate them.

jokes.text: I didn't think you could detect...abnormal behavior among your own kind.
jokes.setup: Jerry surprised his parents notice the Costanzas' dysfunction
jokes.punchline: Comparing elderly people to a separate species
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jerry observation treating age groups like different species with their own behavioral norms.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My parents think Frank and Estelle are crazy. They're like a different species to them.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Old people look at other old people and think, 'What is wrong with those people?'

jokes.text: A bit of a close talker. A what? You'll see.
jokes.setup: Jerry warning about Aaron being a close talker
jokes.punchline: Setting up visual payoff when Aaron demonstrates this
jokes.explanation: Good setup for visual comedy and connects to opening theme. Creates anticipation.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's a close talker. It's like he's trying to get inside your head.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: What do you mean, close talker?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean when he talks to you, he stands right up on top of you. You can feel his breath on your face.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Oh, I hate that.

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: Hey Jerry! How ya doing?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Fine, fine...

jokes.text: They're exhausting. It's like being in an asylum.
jokes.setup: Describing the Costanzas
jokes.punchline: Mental hospital comparison
jokes.explanation: Helen's uncharacteristic harsh assessment. The asylum comparison is vivid and memorable.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Aaron standing uncomfortably close to Jerry's parents while talking
jokes.setup: Previous setup about Aaron being a close talker
jokes.punchline: Audience sees the uncomfortable proximity
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual payoff to earlier setup. Physical comedy that connects to the episode's theme.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm standing there, and he's getting closer and closer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How close?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I could count his nose hairs.

jokes.text: Yeah, that will happen.
jokes.setup: Aaron suggesting Jerry might catch up at the museum
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic acknowledgment that Jerry has no intention of going
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry sarcasm delivered with perfect timing and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'll catch up with you at the museum.

jokes.text: Sorry, pal. Wish I could help you out.
jokes.setup: Jerry desperately calling for Rachel
jokes.punchline: Talking to the answering machine like it's a person
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Jerry's frustrated anthropomorphizing of technology.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Rachel? Rachel, are you there? Pick up, pick up! Come on, Rachel, I know you're there!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look, I don't know if you're screening your calls or what, but this is important. This is really important.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello? Are you listening to me right now? Because I feel like you're listening to me.

jokes.text: Again with the pepper? What do you gotta use all that pepper for? Keep quiet. You trying to set my mouth on fire?
jokes.setup: Costanzas preparing dinner
jokes.punchline: Immediate bickering over seasoning
jokes.explanation: Perfect Costanza domestic warfare - petty but intense arguing over nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Where's the salt? I need salt!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: You don't need salt, you need to watch your blood pressure!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Don't tell me what I need! Pass the salt!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: I'm not passing anything. You'll have a stroke right here at this table.

jokes.text: Don't you think he had to be...nearsighted? Nobody would paint like that if they could see. It's all out of focus.
jokes.setup: Discussing Impressionist paintings
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of artistic style as vision problem
jokes.explanation: Clever misunderstanding of artistic technique as medical condition. Good observational humor.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: You know, I've been looking at these Impressionist paintings, and I think I need glasses.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? You don't need glasses.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Frank, these paintings are all blurry! Either the artist couldn't see, or I can't!

jokes.text: He's from the Impressionist school, you know...like Monet, Manet, Tippy-tippy-day-day.
jokes.setup: Morty trying to sound knowledgeable about art
jokes.punchline: Adding nonsense name to list of famous artists
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Morty moment - fake expertise revealed by ridiculous made-up artist name. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You know, I've been reading about art. Impressionism, cubism, all that stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I'm familiar with all the greats. Picasso, Monet, Van Gogh, Rembrandt... Schleffler.

jokes.text: Hey, you must be Aaron. I've heard about you.
jokes.setup: Aaron introducing himself to Kramer
jokes.punchline: Kramer's response implies negative reputation
jokes.explanation: Good Kramer moment suggesting Aaron's behavior is already notorious.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know who you are.

jokes.text: Why would they? Nobody bought them then.
jokes.setup: Morty saying his coats haven't been made in 20 years
jokes.punchline: Helen's blunt assessment of their commercial failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Helen deflation of Morty's business nostalgia with brutal honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Well, maybe that's because nobody wants them.

jokes.text: This is like the meeting of Smith and Wesson.
jokes.setup: Morty and Rudy forming business partnership
jokes.punchline: Comparing clothing dealers to gun manufacturers
jokes.explanation: Great Kramer hyperbole treating mundane business meeting as historic weapons partnership.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, it's like the gun manufacturers. They're not responsible for what people do with the guns.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, we're like the gun manufacturers. We make the clothes, we're not responsible for what people do with them.

jokes.text: The Eiffel Towers.
jokes.setup: Alec asking where George will stay in Paris
jokes.punchline: George's made-up apartment complex name
jokes.explanation: Perfect George improvisation - pluralizing the Eiffel Tower shows his poor lying skills.

dialogue.speaker: Alec
dialogue.line: Where will you stay in Paris?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm staying at the Chateau de la Salle de Bains.

jokes.text: George, you have no idea how fantastic this is. Fantastic? Yeah.
jokes.setup: George thinking his scheme is working
jokes.punchline: Alec's enthusiasm reveals George's plan is backfiring
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - George's scheme creating exactly what he wanted to avoid.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's working. She's actually interested in me.

dialogue.speaker: Alec
dialogue.line: Yeah, she seems really into you!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, why are you so excited about this?

dialogue.speaker: Alec
dialogue.line: Because now I can finally ask her out!

jokes.text: They bought me a Coke.
jokes.setup: Elaine questioning Aaron's day with elderly strangers
jokes.punchline: Innocent detail that makes the situation more bizarre
jokes.explanation: The childlike detail makes Aaron's behavior even more strange and endearing.

jokes.text: So I took out my belt, just to threaten him...and I got a glimpse of myself in the mirror.
jokes.setup: Explaining how he invented the beltless trench coat
jokes.punchline: Accidentally discovering fashion while threatening child abuse
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - fashion innovation born from threatened violence. Classic Seinfeld moral ambiguity.

dialogue.speaker: Morty Seinfeld
dialogue.line: I'm in the kitchen, I got no belt, my pants are falling down. So I took a trench coat, I cut it up, I tied it around my waist. My wife comes in, she says, 'What are you doing?' I said, 'I'm inventing fashion!' She says, 'That's not fashion, that's a cry for help.' But look at me now!

jokes.text: Jack, it's Morty. Who died?
jokes.setup: Morty calling Jack Klompus
jokes.punchline: Jack's assumption that calls mean death news
jokes.explanation: Perfect elderly character moment - assuming all calls are death notifications. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Jack, hi, it's Morty. Listen, I'm calling because I wanted to tell you something.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Morty? What happened? Is someone dead?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: No, no, nobody's dead. I just wanted to talk to you about something.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Then why are you calling me? You only call me when someone dies!

jokes.text: I guarantee you, Doris won't let him mail those boxes.
jokes.setup: Jack agreeing to mail Morty's boxes
jokes.punchline: Helen's knowledge of other couples' power dynamics
jokes.explanation: Good insight into elderly marriage dynamics and Helen's understanding of spousal control.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: You know, I was talking to Evelyn the other day, and she was telling me that Morty never helps her with anything. And I said, 'Morty helps me all the time.' And then I thought about it, and I realized I was lying.

jokes.text: Maybe it was a mouse. Okay, that's it! We're moving!
jokes.setup: Noise in the attic discussion
jokes.punchline: Frank's extreme overreaction to mouse possibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank Costanza overreaction - zero to moving house over hypothetical mouse.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: There's a noise in the attic.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: A noise? What kind of noise?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know, like a scratching sound. Could be mice.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Mice?! MICE?! We're gonna die! We're all gonna die! The mice are gonna get us! I'm gonna go up there and I'm gonna kill 'em all!

jokes.text: I will not tolerate infestation.
jokes.setup: Frank's mouse phobia
jokes.punchline: Treating one theoretical mouse as full infestation
jokes.explanation: Great Frank vocabulary and catastrophic thinking - one mouse becomes Biblical plague.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: There's a mouse in the apartment!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: We have to get out of here. We have to leave immediately.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Frank, it's just one mouse.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: One mouse? One mouse?! If there's one mouse, there's a hundred mice!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: That's how they work. They scout it out. One mouse comes in, he goes back, tells all his friends.

jokes.text: Could you make it 225? That was his high game in bowling.
jokes.setup: Rudy offering $200 for clothes
jokes.punchline: George using dead father's bowling score for negotiation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George moment - exploiting dead parent sentiment for $25. Perfect mix of pathetic and calculating.

dialogue.speaker: Rudy
dialogue.line: I'll give you two hundred dollars for the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Two hundred? My father bowled a 247. A 247!

dialogue.speaker: Rudy
dialogue.line: What does that have to do with anything?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He was very proud of that score. I think he'd want more than two hundred dollars.

jokes.text: I'm just selling some of Dad's things. That's what he would have wanted.
jokes.setup: George explaining his presence at used clothing store
jokes.punchline: Rationalization for profit from father's death
jokes.explanation: Classic George self-deception and false nobility about obviously selfish behavior.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My father's dead. He's gone. But his wardrobe lives on. I'm not saying it's right, I'm not saying it's wrong, I'm just saying it's happening.

jokes.text: They had plans, huh? They had plans!
jokes.setup: Learning the Seinfelds had dinner with Kramer
jokes.punchline: George's building paranoid repetition
jokes.explanation: Good George paranoia escalation - the repetition shows his obsessive nature.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: They had dinner with Kramer? They had dinner with Kramer? What did they talk about? What could they possibly have to talk about?

jokes.text: These the same people you live with? Yes. Absolutely.
jokes.setup: George defending his parents as wonderful
jokes.punchline: Jerry's pointed reminder of George's living situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry logic - if they're so wonderful, why is adult George living with them?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My parents are wonderful people.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you're living in their house.

jokes.text: Is that a coincidence?
jokes.setup: George's elaborate coincidence story about Joey's father
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic recognition of obvious lie
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry deadpan calling out George's obvious fabrication with minimal words.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And this all happened by coincidence?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are the odds?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, what are the odds.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Aaron standing too close to everyone in the office
jokes.setup: Established close talker behavior
jokes.punchline: Seeing the physical comedy of personal space violation
jokes.explanation: Consistent visual comedy payoff showing Aaron's invasive behavior in professional setting.

dialogue.speaker: The Close Talker
dialogue.line: So I was thinking we could grab coffee sometime.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, that sounds... great.

dialogue.speaker: The Close Talker
dialogue.line: I'm really looking forward to it.

jokes.text: He looks better on TV.
jokes.setup: Seeing Regis Philbin in person
jokes.punchline: Disappointing reality versus television appearance
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about celebrity reality versus image, though somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You know, he's much shorter in person.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What do you expect? He's on television.

jokes.text: My idiot son could open that garage door. What did I do?
jokes.setup: Jack struggling with garage door
jokes.punchline: Comparing Jerry unfavorably to garage door operation
jokes.explanation: Good Jack Klompus insult - casually calling Jerry an idiot while asking for help.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I can't get this garage door to work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Have you tried the remote?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Of course I tried the remote. You know, you're about as useful as this garage door.

jokes.text: A raincoat salesman. I could buy and sell him, like that.
jokes.setup: Frank's anger at the Seinfelds
jokes.punchline: Claiming financial superiority while unemployed and living with adult son
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank delusion - claiming wealth while in obvious financial distress.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I'm the man of this house! I make the money! I pay the bills!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Dad, you're not working.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: That's irrelevant! I still have more money than you!

jokes.text: A mouse! I saw a mouse! It's the remote.
jokes.setup: Frank's established mouse phobia
jokes.punchline: Mistaking TV remote for mouse in panic
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy callback - Frank's paranoia causing false alarm with mundane object.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: There's a mouse in here! A mouse!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: Frank, where? I don't see anything.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Right there! Right there on the table!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: That's the remote control, Frank.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: The remote? Are you sure?

jokes.text: I'm not crazy about used clothes. I mean, they call it vintage...you know, to take your mind off what it really is, nasty-wear.
jokes.setup: Jerry's standup about used clothing
jokes.punchline: Coining 'nasty-wear' as honest term for vintage clothes
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor with clever wordplay. 'Nasty-wear' is a memorable coinage.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what they call used clothes now? 'Vintage.' Vintage! It's just old stuff. I have stuff in my closet that's not old, it's just ugly. But if I wait long enough, it becomes vintage, and suddenly I can charge money for it. We should be honest about it. Call it what it is: nasty-wear. 'Oh, I'm wearing nasty-wear today.' At least then you know what you're getting.

jokes.text: It actually becomes underwear vapor. We don't even throw it out. We just open a window...and it goes out like dandelion spores.
jokes.setup: Men wearing underwear until it disintegrates
jokes.punchline: Poetic description of underwear becoming airborne particles
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist imagery - treating underwear disintegration as natural phenomenon. Very quotable and original.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Men wear underwear until it just disintegrates. Like, you're walking down the street one day, and a light breeze comes along and your underwear just turns into dust and floats away.

jokes.text: The whole movie was in black and white. Oh, yeah, I didn't even realize.
jokes.setup: Testing whether Morty actually saw Schindler's List
jokes.punchline: Morty's obvious lie about not noticing distinctive visual style
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Morty caught in obvious lie about distinctive film characteristic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you saw Schindler's List?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Yeah, I saw it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You didn't notice it was in black and white?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: No, I didn't notice.

jokes.text: Twenty-five percent? It was your idea. Yeah, I know. You're doing all the legwork. That's right. He's ripping you off.
jokes.setup: George stirring up trouble between partners
jokes.punchline: Successfully manipulating Kramer against Morty
jokes.explanation: Good George manipulation showing his talent for creating conflict for personal gain.

jokes.text: All right, let's compromise. Twenty-five percent. Okay, it's a deal.
jokes.setup: Kramer demanding more than 25%, Morty offering less
jokes.punchline: Their 'compromise' is exactly the original deal
jokes.explanation: Excellent circular negotiation joke - all that conflict to reach the same place. Perfect structure.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I want more than 25%.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I can only give you 15%.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Let's compromise. 25%.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: That's what I said in the first place!

jokes.text: Well, I didn't actually get to see the whole movie. Yeah, why not? I was kind of making out.
jokes.setup: Jerry's evasive response about the movie
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of inappropriate behavior
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the episode's most shocking revelation, delivered with Jerry's casual tone.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So what did you think of the movie?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was fine, you know, it had its moments.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Just fine? Come on, what's really going on?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, okay, I may have spent most of it texting under the seat.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? The guy next to me was doing it too!

jokes.text: You were making out during Schindler's List?
jokes.setup: Jerry's casual confession
jokes.punchline: Elaine's horrified reaction to the moral transgression
jokes.explanation: Central joke of the episode - perfect moral outrage at Jerry's inappropriate behavior during serious Holocaust film.

jokes.text: He was moving on her like the stormtroopers into Poland.
jokes.setup: Newman describing Jerry's making out
jokes.punchline: Comparing sexual behavior to Nazi invasion using Holocaust imagery
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly inappropriate Newman line - connecting Jerry's behavior to the film's subject matter with shocking metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: He sucks the air right out of the room. It's like a vaccuum in there. He's making out, and he's inhaling her whole face.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: It's like he's trying to suck out her internal organs through her mouth.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Like a little Dracula.

jokes.text: We started up a little during the coming attractions...and the next thing we knew the war was over.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining how the making out happened
jokes.punchline: Treating WWII as timing reference for sexual activity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry rationalization - using the Holocaust timeline as casual reference for personal sexual activity.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So we're making out, and she says to me, 'Was it good for you?' And I say, 'Yeah, it was great.' She says, 'Better than with your last girlfriend?' I said, 'Oh yeah, much better.' She says, 'How much better?' I said, 'Well, let me put it this way — if my last girlfriend was Pearl Harbor, you're the Marshall Plan.'

jokes.text: You stole my shirt, you son of a bitch! Give me that back. Give me that. Give me.
jokes.setup: Frank recognizing his shirt on Kramer
jokes.punchline: Physical altercation over clothing at dinner
jokes.explanation: Classic Frank explosion - from dinner guest to physical confrontation in seconds. Great physical comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: That's my shirt!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, it's not.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I know my own shirt!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You stole my shirt!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I borrowed it.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You never asked!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Give it back!

jokes.text: A mouse! Egad! A mouse! In the bedroom!
jokes.setup: Frank's established mouse phobia
jokes.punchline: Theatrical panic response to actual mouse sighting
jokes.explanation: Payoff to Frank's mouse paranoia arc - his worst fear realized with theatrical response.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: A mouse! A mouse! There's a mouse in the apartment!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: Frank, calm down, it's just a little mouse.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Just a mouse?! I'm calling an exterminator! I'm calling the police! I'm calling the National Guard!

jokes.text: Hey, I got news for you, four-eyes...there's no way you're staying with us in Paris.
jokes.setup: George giving Joey fatherly advice
jokes.punchline: Joey's immediate rejection and insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect reversal - the 'innocent' child revealing his true cruel nature to George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, Joey, you're a good-looking guy. You should try to use that to your advantage.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, you're not a good-looking guy, and you're doing just fine, so what's your excuse?

jokes.text: He said I was dead? That's right. That's what my life is worth to him, $25?
jokes.setup: Learning George claimed his father was dead
jokes.punchline: Frank's hurt at the low monetary value placed on his death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Frank moment - more upset about the low price than about being declared dead. Perfect character priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I'm dead? I'm worth two hundred and fifty dollars?

jokes.text: They need some water. Why? They'll get dehydrated on the plane! Get the Seinfelds some water! Please!
jokes.setup: Aaron's excessive care for Jerry's parents
jokes.punchline: Desperate concern about airplane dehydration
jokes.explanation: Perfect Aaron obsession - treating routine flight as life-threatening situation requiring intervention.

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: You know, flying can be very dehydrating. You should drink plenty of water on the plane.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Aaron, it's a two-hour flight.

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: Exactly! Two hours of recirculated air. Your skin will crack like a desert.

jokes.text: Did you ever notice he stood too close to you when he talked? No, I hadn't noticed.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking about Aaron's close talking
jokes.punchline: Jerry's obliviousness to behavior that bothered everyone else
jokes.explanation: Good ironic ending - Jerry unaware of the behavior that defined the episode's opening.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He stands too close when he talks to you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean? He seems fine to me.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, he's practically breathing down your neck!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I never noticed. Maybe that's just how he is.

jokes.text: ATMs and urinals. I guess whenever someone's taking valuables...out of their pants, you wanna give them as much room as possible.
jokes.setup: Discussing personal distance at ATMs and urinals
jokes.punchline: Connecting both situations through 'valuables from pants' concept
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational connection tying together seemingly unrelated situations with perfect logic and callback to opening.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I don't understand? Why do people stand so close to you at the ATM? There's this invisible line at the urinal—you don't cross it. But at the ATM, people are practically breathing down your neck.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I think it's because at the urinal, you're protecting your valuables. At the ATM, you're protecting your valuables too—just different valuables. But the principle is the same!

tmdb_overview: Kramer plans to profit from raincoats created by Jerry's dad; George sells his dad's old "cruisewear."
insight: Parents' visit collides with awkward sexual tension in Seinfeld's densest bottle episode.
